displayTitle: 2025-09-10 - Finance and Expenditure Committee - Financial Markets (Conduct of Institutions) Amendment (Duty to Provide Financial Services) Amendment Bill
topic: Financial Markets (Conduct of Institutions) Amendment (Duty to Provide Financial Services) Amendment Bill
description: Wednesday 23 July 2025 | Finance and Expenditure Committee Financial Markets (Conduct of Institutions) Amendment (Duty to Provide Financial Services) Amendment Bill Andy Foster New Zealand Banking Association - Te Rangapu Peke Roger Beaumont, CEO Sam Schuyt, Policy Director and Legal Counsel Te Kahui Inihua o Aotearoa Insurance Council of New Zealand Kris Faafoi, Chief Executive Susan Ivory, Regulatory Affairs Manager Legislation Design and Advisory Committee Simon Peart, Member Karl Simpson, Member Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment Simon Upton, Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment Matt Paterson, Chief Advisor – Policy and Programmes | Kaitohu Matua Kaupapahere me ngā Hōtaka
descriptionSummary: Financial Markets (Conduct of Institutions) Amendment (Duty to Provide Financial Services) Amendment Bill. Andy Foster. New Zealand Banking Association - Te Rangapu Peke

chunks.text: Question and clarification, as you always know, that will be much appreciated. Absolutely. Because we've got some organizations, as you know, um, presenting after you, um, scheduled for 1240. 1240 onwards. Thank you. Well, thank you Mr. Chair. And, and thank you, uh, committee members and, uh, also officials. Um, I'm sure both, uh, now and, and in the future.

In terms of the interaction, I'm sure that we're gonna have a look at the heart. Uh, this bill, um, seeks to do what it says in the title, and I would say that there is something in the title. There is a, uh, in terms of the, the lead in, uh, description, uh, of what the bill's about in terms of talking about banking, actually a financial institution, when you go to the, um, the wording which would change in the legislation, a financial institution is obviously broader than, um, just banks.

It also includes insurance, insurers and non-bank, um, uh, uh, financial organizations as well. So it's important to recognize that right up front. Um, this, uh, bill is about the ability to access financial services. And I think we all would recognize that financial, the ability to access financial services is essential in a modern society.

And that is whether it's to have a bank account, uh, whether it's potentially to have a credit card, whether it's to be able to borrow, to buy property, whether it's to be able to ensure that property, uh, whether it's to be able to run a borrow, to run a business. All of those things are essential. And effectively what it is, is the ability to participate in our modern economy. Uh, the financial markets conduct Act 2013 governs financial institutions, which it defines, uh, in section 4, 4 6 E as a registered bank, a licensed insurer,

or a licensed non-bank deposit taker, which are in the business of providing one or more of those services. And, um, that's further defined in the financial services, uh, service providers registration and disputes resolution Act 2005, which clearly, uh, uh, includes and defines I insurers. So I thought I wanted to make that very clear that insurance is part of this as well.

And actually, I have heard from people about the ability to access insurance as well as access, um, uh, banking services. Um, Mr. Chair, our, our financial institutions are intended to have a duty to provide customers with financial services. And I've described what those are, um, when we talk about a good financial reason.

And, um, uh, Deborah, um, uh, you asked the, um, not in this public session. I didn't so you can't refer to it, please. Oh, and well, you just asked. Oh, sorry. No, you're right. Okay. Yeah. Okay. I won't then. Yes, fair enough. Fair enough too. 'cause that last bit was not in public, was it? So this is, yeah. Okay. Fair enough.

Yeah, we're now, so look, what is a good, I think I'll, I'll answer the prospective question about what a good financial reason is. Um, a good financial reason is that a customer or prospective customer represents an undue risk to the financial institution. Most of notably, for example, if you lend money to someone, you wanna be able to get that money back.

You wanna be able to get the, um, uh, any interest that you're charging on that money back. So it's the ability to be able to recover that. Or if it was an insurance situation, it's the ability to be able to get, uh, you, you not put yourself at too greater risk. And of course, that's something which, um, this committee's already given some attention to in terms of climate change, um, and, uh, managed retreat and all those sort of things.

Because there are gonna be times when it is not sensible for an insurer to provide insurance in some circumstances. 'cause the risk is simply due high. And that is a perfectly valid, uh, financial reason, um, for declining services. So the bill recognizes that. Um, and, but what I will shortly outline is this Bill says it's not reasonable to decline financial services for purely non-financial reasons.

And to stress, and I would stress this to legal entities, to people doing legal business, if you're doing something illegal, completely different. But if you're doing things which are legal, and for example, um, the sex industry is one of those where people have contacted me, that is a legal industry. That is something that Parliament has specifically legalized and should therefore be able to operate legally and be supported to operate legally by financial institutions.

So the reasons could be, uh, that a bank might de bank you 'cause it doesn't like you or your business. That could be environmental reasons, could be social reasons, could be political reasons. And we even had the leader of a, what is now a rather popular party in the United Kingdom are being de banked by, um, some, I think it was NatWest in that case, uh, saying you effectively don't like your politics, therefore debunking you.

That is a pretty dangerous place to go. I spend a bit of time, um, in preparing for this, looking at the analysis of two recent high court cases, which have incidentally also highlighted in the mb uh, briefing. One was Tiger Capital Limited versus Westpac, New Zealand. And the other was the Christian Church Community Trust, AKA Gloria Vale, uh, versus the Bank of New Zealand.

Both of those sought interim injunctions against the termination of banking services target's. Uh, applica injunction was declined. Gloria Vale succeeded. And then the Gloria Vale case was then appealed by the BNZ, uh, to the Court of Appeal, where, um, they did say, well, no, you, the BNZ can decline, um, services. It's interesting to read a Chapman trip article published in the New Zealand Law Journal in October of 2023, which referred to those two high court cases concerning the bank's refusal to provide banking services.

And there's a couple of little bits that I wanted to highlight the article notes as the end quote. As these cases highlight, banks are facing an increasing tension in their social license to operate. And I note that this committee has spent a lot of time looking at the banking industry recently. Back to the quote. On one hand, they're the gateway to necessary banking services to allow customers to participate in modern society.

On the other hand, they have a whole lot of different, it goes on to talk about the different responsibilities that banks have. For example, anti-money laundering, Russian Sanctions Act, and so on. Chapman trip, go on to conclude with competing high court decisions. In this case, two high court decisions went in different directions, neither of which needed to make final decisions on the law, and neither of which referred to another case, Eran the law.

And, and this is an important, but the law is left in a state of uncertainty. That's the current law is left in a state of uncertainty. In the light of the increasing role of banks as frontline as the frontliner of matters such as sanctions, A-M-L-C-F-T, and ESG, it is likely that banks will be making decisions on de banking customers with increasing regularity.

This uncertainty is likely to create issues for banks and their customers. So whatever we may think about what's in this proposed bill, there's quite clearly the analysis which has been done there by Chapman Trip is saying, there is an issue here. We need to address that. And then Minter Allison and, and analyzing the Court of appeal decisions. That was the BNZ appealing against the glory of our one included the following conclusion, de banking is an issue for parliament and regulators.

De banking is an issue for parliament and regulators. The court held that the risk of the glory of our entities being unable to open accounts with other b, other New Zealand banks, which I think was the case in other, was being de banked by BNZ was being de banked effectively by all of 'em. 'cause nobody else wanted to touch them, was insufficient to uphold the injunction and force BNZ to maintain their relationship.

'cause that was part of the original injunction. Justice Goddard, um, in Obiter comments suggested that such broader public interest issues are for Parliament to address not B Z's responsibilities. It opines on whether BNZ had a public interest obligation to provide minimal banking facilities to customers without alternatives.

But this line of argument was not resolved. Uncertainty again, it also notes that while some jurisdictions and impose such obligations by statute, again, other countries doing different things, New Zealand does not. And the court did not find it arguable under common law. In response, the Minister of Finance, the honorable Nicola Willis, published a revised policy remit for the Reserve Bank of New Zealand together with a new letter of expectations. And in that letter, the Minister states, and it's not quite going to the all of, to dealing with the right to have a bank account, but she expects the Reserve Bank to work with wider government to support the banking industry, to take actionable steps towards making basic bank accounts more widely available, ensuring that every New Zealander has access

to essential banking services without unnecessary impediments. So of course, that doesn't enshrine the right to a bank account, but it certainly sends in certain, uh, direction. I would note that the initial briefing also noted that de banking is not just an issue in New Zealand. for political or religious reasons. So New Zealand is not unique in this. Now, I'm sure that there will be plenty of argument to say that this does not happen. There is no de banking. Uh, as set out in the general policy statement, there are concerns that banks are withdrawing services for political views and wokeness.

Given some of the media headlines I've seen, I can understand how that perception, uh, has arisen. This bill is based on a serious misunderstanding. It ignores the fact that banks make credit decisions based on commercial reasons, not on the basis of murky moralizing.

The reality is that no industry or customer group is being Deb banked. As part of some murky conspiracy, some individual banks have made individual commercial decisions to not lend to a business, but banks have not done this as a collective.

Uh, and this is not the first time authorities have looked into these issues. The Commerce Commission has investigated and found that there is no cozy cartel of banks operating under the net zero banking alliance, despite accusations to the contrary. Uh, last year's commerce commission market study, and this very committee's recent year long inquiry into banking competition were comprehensive studies of the industry and neither identified woke de banking as an issue.

That's because it isn't one. Banks are making, uh, each making commercial decisions on a case by case basis based on regulatory requirements and their individual risk appetite. Banks may assess their exposure to sectors in the loan book as part of their ongoing overall risk management, and this may mean they limit further lending to either an individual business or increase lending to another, but each, excuse me.

But each bank decides that separately, and each bank has a different loan book and a different risk appetite. And depending on market conditions, they may well change their view. Again, sometimes a bank, uh, does get this wrong. Uh, not continuing to lend to video rental companies turned out to be the right commercial decision, but stopping credit to bookstores was not.

It's a fine line. And banks, like any business, make their own commercial decisions and risk appetite decisions every single day. And banks are not making these calls for wokeness reasons or political favor. Uh, this is obvious because banks aren't promoting these decisions.

The news stories come from disgruntled customers and their backers or advocates. If wokeness was the reason to limit lending banks, media teams might well be issuing press releases, seeking favorable coverage. I have the utmost sympathy for customers who can no longer obtain credit from their bank and may be struggling to find another lender to give them finance.

Sometimes that is simply the reality of the financial state of that company or the customer, rather than some conspiracy against a particular sector. Our member banks acknowledge that banking is a fundamental service and are supporting the Reserve Bank's policy work on enhancing access to banking alongside the recent anti-money laundering changes.

So, despite the unfounded perception, this bill isn't fixing an actual problem. However, if it were to become law, it could create a range of problems by creating more bureaucracy and regulatory uncertainty in the lending process, this bill could slow down access to credit for legitimate businesses and customers who would normally get their lending easily approved.

The threat of jail time may influence a banker's decision making, uh, and the credit outcome for that business or customer as existing banking conduct legislation already protects New Zealanders from, uh, unjustifiable refusal or withdrawal of banking services.

Uh, this government is focused on cutting red tape and economic growth. This bill would be a blow to both goals and undermine the principle that businesses should determine who they do business with. The banking industry is working hard to ensure it delivers on recommendations from the Commerce Commission, this select committee, and other important regulatory changes such as open banking and triple CFA reform.

Uh, this committee's inquiry correctly identified that one barrier to lending to certain sectors is the risk weighting requirements that are set for sectors such as agriculture. The Reserve Bank's consultation on, on its review of capital setting settings proposes to lower some of those risk weights.

This could encourage more lending to those sectors if the proposals go through these work streams should smooth the path to easier banking, easier access to banking, more streamlined credit processes, and improved economic growth. This bill gets in the way of those processes for those reasons, we oppose it and we are now happy to take your questions. Okay, thank you Mr.

That's because acting in accordance with ESG type considerations is generally considered to be a valid commercial strategy. And thirdly, the bill is a poor fit with the Financial Markets Conduct Act. That ACT uses civil enforcement for most of its contraventions. And the proposed definitions, as have been discussed, are inconsistent with definitions in the Financial Markets Conduct Act.

So I'll pass on to Simon to cover some things in more detail, and then we'll leave some time for questions. Uh, so just to expand slightly on the points that, uh, that Carl has made, um, starting with the significantly coercive nature of the obligation, um, it is very unusual in New Zealand law to create an obligation to supply services, even essential utility services.

Um, you think, for example, electricity line services, which is about as close as you could get to an essential service. We have, um, consumer protection requirements around particularly medically dependent consumers, but there is no generic obligation to continue supplying or to take on new customers for electricity.

Uh, to the extent that New Zealand law has provided in the past for continuing obligations to supply, it's tended to be in the context of privatization of previously publicly provided services. So, for example, pre 1993, um, electricity connections. So this would be a reasonably significant extension of the, you know, coercive power, um, to require the provision of these services.

Um, secondly, you have heard and will hear a lot about, um, the uncertain scope, uh, of the obligations. In our advice. We focus principally on the tension between the primary prohibition, uh, and the exception for valid and, um, and verifiable commercial reasons.

Um, as Carla said, for for the most part, uh, when a company is having regard to ESG considerations, it is doing so for commercial purposes, uh, because it is determined that it is in its commercial interests, in the interests of the company and its shareholders to take account of those matters, either because those considerations are specific to the commercial strategy that it's pursuing, uh, or because there will be negative, uh, impacts on the company if it does not have regard to those considerations.

Um, so there is an inherent tension between, on the one hand, prohibiting having regard to those matters. And on the other hand, saying that it's legitimate to have regard to valid and verifiable commercial reasons from a sort of legislative design perspective, when we're trying to resolve that tension, it leaves you in really one of two situations.

Um, either we are saying that, uh, you know, having regard to ESG matters is, you know, a valid commercial consideration, in which case the prohibition has little or no effect. Uh, or alternatively, we're saying that having regard to ESG considerations as effectively deemed to be a non-commercial consideration, in which case the exception has little or no effect.

Um, and that tension has to be resolved in order for this to be a, a workable regime. Um, there are another other, a number of other aspects of uncertainty and the drafting.
